title: Death rate from road injuries
subtitle: The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.
caption:
Death rate from road injuries

The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.

This choropleth map displays the global distribution of road injury mortality rates for the year 2021, using a continuous color scale to represent the annual number of deaths per 100,000 people. The legend defines the range from 0 to 70, where lighter beige tones indicate lower mortality rates and progressively darker shades of orange and red signify higher frequencies of fatal road incidents. The visualization reveals a distinct geographic concentration of the highest values across parts of Africa and the Middle East. Algeria and Mauritania appear in the darkest orange bands, placing them in the upper tiers of the scale, likely between 30 and 50 deaths per 100,000. In contrast, the lowest rates are visible across Western Europe, including Spain, France, and the United Kingdom, as well as in Canada, Australia, and Japan, where the map shows the lightest beige shading corresponding to the 0 to 10 range. The United States and Brazil sit in an intermediate band, darker than Western Europe but lighter than the highest-risk nations, falling roughly within the 10 to 20 range. Large portions of the Russian Federation and China also display moderate shading, indicating rates higher than the global minimum but lower than the peaks seen in North Africa. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching to denote that no data is available for that region. The overall pattern shows that the burden of road injury deaths is not evenly distributed, with the most intense colors clustered in specific regions of the Global South while high-income nations in Europe and Oceania consistently occupy the lowest end of the spectrum.
